verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: There has been from the first in the churches of God, and especially in that of Jerusalem, a plentiful supply of men who being born Jews have become Christians; and their perfect acquaintance with both languages and their sufficient knowledge of the law is shewn by their administration of the pontifical office.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: In all this abundance of learned men, has there been one who has dared to make havoc of the divine record handed down to the Churches by the Apostles and the deposit of the Holy Spirit?

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: For what can we call it but havoc, when some parts of it are transformed, and this is called the correction of an error?

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: For instance, the whole of the history of Susanna, which gave a lesson of chastity to the churches of God, has by him been cut out, thrown aside and dismissed.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: The hymn of the three children, which is regularly sung on festivals in the Church of God, he has wholly erased from the place where it stood.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: But why should I enumerate these cases one by one, when their number cannot be estimated?

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: This, however, cannot be passed over.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: The seventy translators, each in their separate cells, produced a version couched in consonant and identical words, under the inspiration, as we cannot doubt, of the Holy Spirit; and this version must certainly be of more authority with us than a translation made by a single man under the inspiration of Barabbas.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: But, putting this aside, I beg you to listen, for example, to this as an instance of what we mean.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: Peter was for twenty-four years Bishop of the Church of Rome.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: We cannot doubt that, amongst other things necessary for the instruction of the church, he himself delivered to them the treasury of the sacred books, which, no doubt, had even then begun to be read under his presidency and teaching.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: What are we to say then?

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: Did Peter the Apostle of Christ deceive the church and deliver to them books which were false and contained nothing of truth?

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: Are we to believe that he knew that the Jews possessed what was true, and yet determined that the Christians should have what was false?

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: But perhaps the answer will be made that Peter was illiterate, and that, though he knew that the books of the Jews were truer than those which existed in the church, yet he could not translate them into Latin because of his linguistic incapacity.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: Was the tongue of fire given by the Holy Spirit from heaven of no avail to him?

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: Did not the Apostles speak in all languages?
